title: Burdock root decoction
subtitle: An traditional cleansing herb herbal decoction for spring skin and lymphatic support
excerpt: Dried burdock root simmered for 15-20 minutes. A traditional traditional cleansing herb herb taken in spring for sluggish skin, mild acne-prone complexion, and general stagnation after winter.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, burdock root entry: the traditional cleansing herb, diuretic, and skin tradition. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Arctium lappa, the Eclectic use for skin and lymphatic conditions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried burdock root stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark. Prepare each cup fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A traditional herbal category describing herbs that gradually improve tissue nutrition and waste removal. Alteratives were prescribed in European and American Eclectic medicine for chronic skin conditions, sluggish lymphatic function, and general constitutional improvement.

content.text: Burdock root (Arctium lappa) is one of the classic cleansing herbs of the western tradition.

herbs. The Eclectic physicians used it for chronic skin conditions and sluggish lymphatic function. Maud Grieve, the early 20th-century botanical writer, records it as a traditional spring herb. The simmered preparation is made from the dried root. It tastes slightly sweet, slightly bitter, and earthy.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy. Burdock is in the daisy family. Readers with a known ragweed, chrysanthemum, or daisy allergy should avoid.

Pregnancy. Burdock root is avoided at therapeutic doses in pregnancy. Consult a herbalist or midwife before use.

Burdock root identification. Wild burdock root is foraged by some herbalists; the plant is straightforward to identify when seeding (large basal leaves, hooked seed burs). If you are using foraged root rather than purchased dried root, confirm the identification carefully before use.

items.prepNote: dried and cut burdock root, about 1 heaped teaspoon
cold start; reduces to about 250-300 ml

content.text: Combine root and cold water in a small saucepan. Bring to a gentle simmer and maintain for 15-20 minutes. The liquid should reduce to about 250-300 ml, turn pale amber-brown, and smell slightly sweet and earthy. Strain through a sieve, pressing the spent root. Drink warm before or between meals.

One cup (250 ml, containing 5 g dried root) once or twice daily, before or between meals. Take for 3-4 weeks in spring. the The standard dose range is 2-6 g dried root per dose. If skin symptoms have not improved after 4-6 weeks, consult a herbalist or doctor. Paediatric use: not for children under twelve without specialist advice.
Prepare each cup fresh. Dried burdock root stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy
Known daisy allergy. Pregnancy. Infected or severe skin problems need a doctor.
